one_line_takeaway: A position/vision paper arguing that LLM agents should orchestrate federated learning (server-side selection/bias/security reasoning, client-side privacy/resource guardianship), backed only by a small proof-of-concept showing that LLM-driven client-count selection trades accuracy for large, model- and prompt-dependent selection-time overhead -- not a security paper despite citing agent-security risks (jailbreak, prompt injection, backdoors, memory poisoning) from prior survey work.
key_finding: Across both datasets, the choice of LLM and prompting strategy trades off selection-time overhead against accuracy/K variability rather than producing a clear winner. Qwen3 8b tended to pick lower average K with higher standard deviation and achieved the higher accuracies in the table but at substantially higher Selection Time (e.g., CIFAR-10, Description-Only, Oort baseline: K=12±6, Acc=39±14%, ST=4.45s). Llama3.2 3b produced accuracy comparable to the other models with much lower ST because of shorter reasoning chains (e.g., CIFAR-10, CoT, Random baseline: K=8±2, Acc=36±13%, ST=0.15s). CoT prompting generally gave the lowest ST among prompt types, though with model-dependent exceptions (Llama3.1 8b sometimes had faster Description-Only runs, but those runs produced static/non-adaptive K values). Reported accuracy differences across LLMs/prompts/baselines are largely within one standard deviation of each other (roughly 35-39% on CIFAR-10, roughly 95-97% on MNIST), so the demonstrated effect is primarily on selection-time overhead and K-selection variability, not on accuracy improvement.
core_contribution: This is a position paper (published at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI for Mechanism Design and Strategic Decision Making) that proposes 'Agentic-FL', a paradigm in which LLM-based agents (LM-Agents) take over orchestration roles in Federated Learning (FL): server-side agents perform contextual client selection, aggregation weighting, and bias mitigation, while client-side agents act as local 'guardians' that manage privacy budgets, hyperparameters, and model compression under resource constraints. The paper's own contributions, as stated by the authors, are (1) motivating/arguing for LM-Agents in FL, (2) enumerating open challenges and future directions for agentic FL orchestration, and (3) a small proof-of-concept ('K-Agent') demonstrating an LLM agent deciding the number K of clients to select per round. The bulk of the paper (Sections 3-4) is discursive/position content connecting LM-agent capabilities to known FL problems (synchronization, communication overhead, fault tolerance, bias, security) and citing existing FL/agent literature for each; it is explicitly a vision/roadmap paper, not a system evaluated at scale.
threat_model: Not applicable in the sense of a proposed or evaluated threat model — this paper does not attack or defend anything. It contains a discussion subsection ('Security', Section 4) that catalogs security concerns for agentic FL as an open research direction: it explicitly attributes the enumerated risks (LLM jailbreaking, prompt injection into the agent, training-time backdoors, memory poisoning by malicious clients, and privacy leakage via improper information extraction) to a cited external survey ('Miao Yu et al.' / Yu et al. 2025), not to the authors' own analysis. The paper also separately discusses FL-native security issues (gradient inversion, model poisoning, differential-privacy/robust-aggregation trade-offs) again via citations (Gao et al. 2025; Ma et al. 2022; Yurdem et al. 2024), proposing only the qualitative idea that an LM-agent could 'dynamically manage the privacy budget' or 'distinguish statistical anomalies from malicious attacks' — no mechanism, algorithm, or experiment for this is given.
limitations: The authors' own proof-of-concept only tests one narrow piece of the proposed Agentic-FL vision (LLM-driven choice of the client count K); the much larger set of claimed capabilities in the position sections -- bias mitigation via semantic client-metadata reasoning, dynamic privacy-budget management, poisoning/gradient-inversion detection, fault-tolerance recovery, mechanism-design-based incentive negotiation -- are proposed conceptually but not implemented or evaluated anywhere in the paper. The PoC is small-scale (25 simulated clients, 50 rounds, single CNN architecture) and reports accuracy differences that are mostly within the reported standard deviations, so it does not demonstrate an accuracy benefit from agentic selection, only a characterization of LLM-inference time overhead versus model size/prompt strategy. The security content is entirely a literature-derived risk catalog (jailbreaking, prompt injection, backdoors, memory poisoning, privacy leakage), not a threat model, attack, or defense that this paper itself develops or tests -- readers should not treat Section 4's 'Security' subsection as a novel security contribution.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (Dirichlet distribution, alpha=0.1, 25 clients, 50 communication rounds)
MNIST (Dirichlet distribution, alpha=0.1, 25 clients, 50 communication rounds)
quantitative_results: CIFAR-10, Qwen3 8b, Description-Only, Power-of-Choice baseline: K avg 7±5, Accuracy 38±13%, Selection Time 3.79s
CIFAR-10, Qwen3 8b, Description-Only, Oort baseline: K avg 12±6, Accuracy 39±14%, Selection Time 4.45s
CIFAR-10, Llama3.2 3b, CoT, Random baseline: K avg 8±2, Accuracy 36±13%, Selection Time 0.15s
CIFAR-10, Llama3.1 8b, Description-Only, Power-of-Choice baseline: K avg 10±0, Accuracy 37±13%, Selection Time 0.18s
MNIST, Qwen3 8b, Description-Only, Oort baseline: K avg 11±6, Accuracy 97.06±1%, Selection Time 4.45s
MNIST, Llama3.1 8b, Few-Shot, Oort baseline: K avg 9±1, Accuracy 97.21±1%, Selection Time 0.33s
MNIST, Llama3.2 3b, CoT, Random baseline: K avg 8±2, Accuracy 96.25±2%, Selection Time 0.18s
baselines_compared: Round Robin (named in text as a compared method but not shown with its own columns in the results table)
